episode.title: Cariboo Signals - Science, Wonder & the Natural World
episode.description:
Riley and Casey explore technology and society in rural communities. Today's focus: Science, Wonder & the Natural World.

NEWS ROUNDUP: We break down [Business Insider] The AI industry's profit problem is getting bigger, says Goldman Sachs' equity research chief and [Business Insider] I flew on a startup's one-of-a-kind electric plane that could reshape air travel by 2027, plus 1 more stories, and explore what these developments mean for communities like ours.

RURAL CONNECTIONS: Can AI tools reshape ecological science in the Cariboo in ways that serve community interests, or do they primarily amplify existing extractive research structures? Topics include: Infrastructure costs of AI and ecological impact of data centres, Indigenous data sovereignty and structural exclusion from peer-reviewed research systems, Edge-deployed vs. cloud-based AI models for environmental monitoring, Community-owned data licensing and fee-for-access ecological knowledge systems, Funding and sustainability models for long-term community AI governance infrastructure.

Hosts: Riley (Tech optimist with an engineering background) and Casey (Community-first skeptic of tech promises).

COMMUNITY SPOTLIGHT: Community Futures Cariboo Chilcotin

articles.title: [Toms Guide] I tested local AI vs. ChatGPT side-by-side — here are the 7 biggest differences
articles.author: https://www.tomsguide.com/author/amanda-caswell
articles.summary: I tested local AI and cloud AI side-by-side to compare privacy, speed, research capabilities, customization and cost — here are the 7 biggest differences and which one wins.

discussion.central_question: Can AI tools reshape ecological science in the Cariboo in ways that serve community interests, or do they primarily amplify existing extractive research structures?
discussion.riley_position: Constrained AI models designed for specific monitoring tasks (acoustic sensors, camera traps, eDNA analysis) could reduce labour costs and enable locally-governed ecological monitoring without requiring massive data centre infrastructure, particularly if training data is controlled by Indigenous nations.
riley_key_evidence: Consensus AI tool that answers only from peer-reviewed research, demonstrating that constrained models have lower infrastructure requirements than foundation models
New Brunswick Lorneville data centre would destroy wetlands and old-growth forest while running ecological models, illustrating the contradiction between AI compute footprint and ecological study
Acoustic monitoring with edge-deployed local models could run without cloud infrastructure or data leaving territory
discussion.casey_position: Community-controlled AI knowledge systems face fundamental governance and sustainability challenges; amplifying peer-reviewed research excludes Indigenous knowledge systematically, and the ongoing labour costs of maintaining such systems fall on already over-resourced communities without genuine co-investment.
casey_key_evidence: Training data for AI models comes from research institutions with their own taxonomic frameworks and access conditions, encoding particular epistemologies that may exclude Indigenous knowledge
Peer-reviewed-only research tools structurally exclude community-governed data that doesn't get published in indexed journals, replicating existing extraction patterns
Communities are already resource-constrained; adding 'maintain a community AI system' without sustained funding is an unfunded mandate with a tech veneer
discussion.resolution: Riley and Casey shifted from debating whether AI helps to identifying the actual structural question: can a community-owned, fee-for-access ecological knowledge system become financially self-sustaining without compromising governance? They agreed the conversation requires bringing economic development organizations, Indigenous nations, and provincial policy-makers together, and acknowledged they hadn't directly consulted affected communities.
topics_covered: Infrastructure costs of AI and ecological impact of data centres
Indigenous data sovereignty and structural exclusion from peer-reviewed research systems
Edge-deployed vs. cloud-based AI models for environmental monitoring
Community-owned data licensing and fee-for-access ecological knowledge systems
Funding and sustainability models for long-term community AI governance infrastructure
